text: Concurrent strength and endurance training produces smaller hypertrophy gains than strength training alone but significantly greater gains than endurance training alone (effect sizes: 0.85 vs 1.23 vs 0.27)

Concurrent training reduces power development more than it reduces strength or hypertrophy, with significant differences between all three conditions (strength-only ES 0.91, concurrent ES 0.55, endurance-only ES 0.11)
Running produces significantly greater interference with strength training adaptations (both hypertrophy and strength) than cycling when performed concurrently with resistance training
Higher frequency of endurance training is associated with greater decrements in hypertrophy, strength, and power when combined with resistance training (correlations -0.26 to -0.35)

Longer duration endurance sessions produce greater interference with resistance training outcomes, with stronger negative effects on power than on hypertrophy or strength (correlations -0.29 to -0.75)
Higher exercise intensity (as measured by percentage of maximal heart rate) during concurrent training is associated with greater reductions in body fat (r = -0.60)
Runners who want to preserve strength and power gains from resistance training should minimize the frequency and duration of their endurance sessions rather than eliminating strength work entirely
